items.q: Why is 'renewable' not the same as 'clean' energy?
items.correct: Renewable means the source replenishes naturally on human timescales; clean means low environmental/health impact. Hydropower is renewable but floods habitats; bioenergy is renewable but can drive deforestation.
items.misconception: All renewable energy sources are clean and have no environmental impact.
items.random_wrong: Clean energy means it doesn't produce any smoke when burned.
items.why: Large dams cause major ecological and social harm despite being renewable. Nuclear is low-carbon but not renewable. The two categories overlap but don't match — important for energy-policy evaluation.

items.correct: Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs — balancing economic, social and environmental goals.
items.misconception: Sustainable development means stopping all economic growth.
items.random_wrong: Sustainable development means using only solar power.
items.why: Students treat sustainability as anti-development. The Brundtland definition is about balance and intergenerational equity — not zero growth or one specific technology.

items.q: What happens during an El Nino event?
items.correct: Trade winds weaken, warm water that normally pools in the western Pacific spreads east, disrupting weather patterns globally — drought in Australia, floods in South America.
items.misconception: El Nino is when the ocean gets colder near Peru.
items.random_wrong: El Nino is the name of a specific hurricane that hit Mexico.
items.why: El Nino brings WARM water to Peru — the cold-water state is La Nina. Students mix up the two phases; the name 'the Christ child' refers to warm currents at Christmas.
It's Meeting needs without harming future. “Brand new” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Amount of CO2 you produce. “A footprint in mud” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Roughly what % of people live in cities globally?
items.why: Exact answer: 55. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
